edHandoff.summary: 63-year-old male presenting with 2 days of palpitations, exertional dyspnea, and lightheadedness. Found in atrial fibrillation with ventricular rate of 142 on arrival. BP 108/72. No chest pain or syncope. Given diltiazem 20mg IV push x1 in ED — rate came down to 112 but still in afib. Started on diltiazem drip at 10mg/hr. TSH and troponin pending. TTE ordered but not yet done. No prior history of afib. On metoprolol at home for HTN but admits he ran out 5 days ago.
edHandoff.disposition: Admit to telemetry for rate control optimization, anticoagulation assessment, workup of new-onset afib, and echocardiogram.

sources.name: 2023 ACC/AHA/ACCP/HRS Guideline for Diagnosis and Management of Atrial Fibrillation

variant.diagnosis: New-Onset Atrial Fibrillation with Rapid Ventricular Response

patient.communicationStyle: Articulate and concerned. Can describe his palpitations well. Anxious about having a 'heart attack.' Feels guilty about running out of his blood pressure medication. Wants to know if this is going to happen again.
patient.emotionalState: Anxious about cardiac diagnosis, cooperative, seeking reassurance

patientPhrasing: feels like it's fluttering in my chest
can't catch my breath when I walk
chiefComplaint.location: Diffuse chest — awareness of heartbeat, fluttering sensation
chiefComplaint.onset: Noticed palpitations 2 days ago. Initially intermittent, became constant yesterday. Dyspnea on exertion worsened progressively.
chiefComplaint.severity: Palpitations 6/10 bothersome. Dyspnea with walking across the room. Lightheaded when standing.
chiefComplaint.timing: Constant, no clear trigger. Ran out of metoprolol 5 days ago and didn't refill it.

present.name: palpitations
present.patientSays: My heart feels like it's fluttering or quivering. It's not a regular thump — it's all over the place. I can feel it in my neck sometimes.

present.name: exertional dyspnea
present.patientSays: I get winded just walking to the bathroom now. That's not normal for me — I can usually walk a couple of blocks without any problem.

present.name: lightheadedness
present.patientSays: I feel dizzy when I stand up. Almost like I'm going to pass out, but I haven't actually fainted.

present.name: fatigue
present.patientSays: I'm wiped out. No energy at all the past two days.

present.name: mild exercise intolerance
present.patientSays: I tried to mow the lawn yesterday and had to stop after five minutes.

absent.name: chest pain
absent.patientSays: No actual pain. Just the fluttering and the breathlessness.

absent.name: orthopnea/PND
absent.patientSays: I can lie flat okay. I don't wake up at night gasping.

absent.name: heat intolerance/tremor
absent.patientSays: No. I haven't noticed any changes like that.

absent.name: recent illness/surgery
absent.patientSays: No, I haven't been sick or had any procedures.

absent.name: alcohol binge
absent.patientSays: I had a couple of beers over the weekend, nothing crazy.

pmh: Hypertension (10 years, usually well-controlled on metoprolol)
Obstructive Sleep Apnea (diagnosed 3 years ago, prescribed CPAP but uses it 'sometimes')

medications.name: Metoprolol succinate
medications.patientSays: I ran out about 5 days ago. I've been meaning to get it refilled but kept putting it off.

medications.name: Metformin
medications.patientSays: I take this for my sugar.

medications.name: CPAP
medications.patientSays: I have one but I don't use it every night. Maybe 3-4 nights a week. My wife says I snore terribly.

socialHistory.smoking: Former — quit 8 years ago (20 pack-year history)
socialHistory.alcohol: 2-3 beers on weekends. Denies binge drinking. No recent heavy use.
socialHistory.occupation: High school principal — stressful job, mostly sedentary
socialHistory.exercise: Minimal — tries to walk but limited by weight and deconditioning

familyHistory: Father: atrial fibrillation (diagnosed in his 60s), stroke at age 72

present: hypertension (most common modifiable risk factor for afib)
obstructive sleep apnea (strongly associated with afib — and undertreated)
recent beta-blocker withdrawal (may have unmasked/triggered afib)

physicalExam.general: Mildly anxious, obese male, sitting up in bed, mild respiratory effort at rest. Comfortable when still.
physicalExam.heent: No thyromegaly. No lid lag or proptosis. Oropharynx: crowded airway (Mallampati III — OSA anatomic risk factor).
physicalExam.neck: JVP approximately 8cm H2O (mildly elevated). No carotid bruits. Visible irregular jugular venous pulsations (absent 'a' waves — lost atrial contraction).
physicalExam.cardiovascular: IRREGULARLY IRREGULAR rhythm. Rate approximately 112. Variable S1 intensity (hallmark of afib). No murmurs. No S3 or S4. No rubs. PMI non-displaced.
physicalExam.pulmonary: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezes, crackles, or rhonchi.
physicalExam.abdominal: Obese. Soft, non-tender, non-distended. No hepatomegaly.
physicalExam.extremities: No peripheral edema. Warm and well-perfused. 2+ pulses throughout.
physicalExam.neurologic: Alert and oriented x4. No focal deficits. No signs of prior stroke.

findings: Atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response at 112 bpm (on diltiazem drip)
Normal QRS duration (no bundle branch block)
Left ventricular hypertrophy by voltage criteria (consistent with longstanding HTN)
ecg.interpretation: Atrial fibrillation with RVR. LVH. No acute ischemic changes.

labs.note: Normal — demand ischemia excluded. Can be mildly elevated in RVR from rate-related supply/demand mismatch.
Moderately elevated — can be elevated in afib due to atrial stretch and rapid rate. Does not necessarily indicate heart failure. Context matters.

labs.value: Na 140, K 4.1, Cl 102, HCO3 24, BUN 18, Cr 1.0, Glucose 148
labs.note: Normal electrolytes. Glucose mildly elevated (known DM).
Low-normal. Replete if targeting rhythm control.
Baseline liver function for anticoagulation consideration

imaging.findings: Mild cardiomegaly. No pulmonary edema or pleural effusions. No focal consolidation or pneumothorax. Mild thoracic kyphosis.
imaging.impression: Mild cardiomegaly, no acute cardiopulmonary process.

imaging.findings: EXPECTED FINDINGS (obtained on hospital day 1): LVEF 50% (low-normal — possible early tachycardia-mediated cardiomyopathy). Mild left atrial dilation (4.3cm). Concentric LVH consistent with hypertensive heart disease. No significant valvular disease. No intracardiac thrombus visualized (TEE would be needed for definitive LA appendage evaluation). Normal RV size and function.
imaging.impression: Mild LV systolic dysfunction (likely rate-related), dilated LA, LVH. No significant valvular disease.

admittingOrders.telemetry: Continuous telemetry monitoring. Target resting HR <110 bpm (lenient rate control per RACE II trial). Alert for: HR >150, pauses >3 seconds, conversion to sinus rhythm, new ST changes.

rateControl.current: Continue diltiazem drip at 10mg/hr. Titrate to HR <110 resting.
rateControl.transition: Once rate controlled on drip for 6-12 hours, transition to oral diltiazem ER 180mg daily OR restart metoprolol succinate 100mg daily (patient's home medication — reason for preference). Do NOT combine beta-blocker and non-dihydropyridine CCB (risk of severe bradycardia/heart block).
rateControl.ifRefractory: If HR remains >120 despite adequate rate control agent, consider adding digoxin 0.25mg IV x1 (especially if LV dysfunction suspected). Check renal function before dosing.
rateControl.notes: Beta-blocker (metoprolol) may be preferred given this patient's comorbidities: HTN, DM, HFpEF/tachycardia-mediated cardiomyopathy. Diltiazem is reasonable bridge while in-house.

anticoagulation.riskAssessment: CHA2DS2-VASc score calculation: C (CHF/LV dysfunction) = 1, H (Hypertension) = 1, A2 (Age 63 — between 65-74 so 1 point) = 1, D (Diabetes) = 1, S2 (no stroke/TIA) = 0, VA (no vascular disease) = 0, Sc (Sex — male) = 0. TOTAL = 4. Strong indication for anticoagulation (score ≥2 in males = recommended).
anticoagulation.bleedingRisk: HAS-BLED score: H (HTN) = 1, A (no abnormal renal/liver) = 0, S (no stroke) = 0, B (no prior bleeding) = 0, L (no labile INR — using DOAC) = 0, E (elderly — 63, borderline) = 0, D (no drugs/alcohol excess) = 0. TOTAL = 1 (low bleeding risk).
anticoagulation.plan: Start apixaban 5mg PO BID (preferred DOAC given renal function and weight). Full-dose apixaban is appropriate — no dose reduction criteria met (age <80, weight >60kg, Cr <1.5). Begin on admission once decision made. DOAC preferred over warfarin for non-valvular afib (lower ICH risk, no INR monitoring).
anticoagulation.alternatives: If DOAC not tolerated or cost-prohibitive: warfarin with bridge heparin drip until INR 2-3. Rivaroxaban 20mg daily with dinner is alternative DOAC.

workup.echocardiogram: TTE if not already obtained — assess LV function, LA size, valvular disease. Critical for management decisions.
workup.sleepApnea: Reinforce CPAP compliance. OSA is a major modifiable risk factor for afib recurrence. Consider sleep medicine consult.
workup.additionalLabs: Magnesium level — replete to >2.0 (stabilizes myocardium). Hepatic panel for anticoagulation baseline.

admittingOrders.monitoring: Vitals q4h. Telemetry continuous. BMP in AM (electrolytes, renal function). Daily weight. I&O if volume status concern.
admittingOrders.diet: Cardiac/diabetic diet. Low sodium. No alcohol.
admittingOrders.dvtProphylaxis: Therapeutic anticoagulation (apixaban) serves as DVT prophylaxis — no additional chemical prophylaxis needed.
admittingOrders.other: Diabetes management: continue home metformin + atorvastatin + lisinopril. Restart metoprolol (after diltiazem drip discontinued — do not overlap). Physical therapy consult for safe mobilization. Smoking cessation reinforcement (already quit). Weight management/nutrition counseling referral. CPAP at bedside nightly.

teaching.correctDiagnosis: New-Onset Atrial Fibrillation with Rapid Ventricular Response
keyLearningPoints: Irregularly irregular rhythm with absent P waves = atrial fibrillation until proven otherwise
Variable S1 intensity on auscultation is a hallmark physical exam finding of afib (variable diastolic filling)
Rate control target: resting HR <110 bpm is acceptable (RACE II trial showed lenient is non-inferior to strict <80 target)
CHA2DS2-VASc score determines anticoagulation need: ≥2 in males (≥3 in females) = strong recommendation. This patient scores 4 — clear indication.
DOACs (apixaban, rivaroxaban) are preferred over warfarin for non-valvular afib: lower ICH risk, no monitoring, fewer interactions
Apixaban dose reduction criteria (2 of 3: age ≥80, weight ≤60kg, Cr ≥1.5) — this patient meets NONE, so full dose 5mg BID
Always check TSH in new-onset afib — hyperthyroidism is a reversible cause
OSA is one of the most underappreciated modifiable risk factors for afib. Untreated OSA increases afib recurrence after cardioversion.
Never combine a beta-blocker with a non-dihydropyridine CCB (diltiazem/verapamil) — risk of severe bradycardia and AV block
BNP can be elevated in afib WITHOUT heart failure — atrial stretch and tachycardia raise BNP. Interpret in clinical context.
mustNotMiss: WPW with afib (wide-complex irregular tachycardia — AV nodal blockers like diltiazem/digoxin are CONTRAINDICATED; use procainamide or cardioversion)
Tachycardia-mediated cardiomyopathy (prolonged uncontrolled rates → LV dysfunction — this patient may have early signs with LVEF 50%)
Acute coronary syndrome as trigger (serial troponins — normal here)
Stroke risk — anticoagulation decision cannot wait. Score the CHA2DS2-VASc on admission.
commonPitfalls: Cardioverting new-onset afib without anticoagulation — if onset >48 hours (or unknown), need 3 weeks of anticoagulation or TEE to rule out LA thrombus before cardioversion
Forgetting to calculate CHA2DS2-VASc — anticoagulation is the single most impactful intervention for reducing stroke in afib
Using aspirin as a 'compromise' instead of anticoagulation — aspirin is NOT recommended for stroke prevention in afib
Combining diltiazem drip with beta-blocker when transitioning — stop the drip BEFORE starting the oral beta-blocker
Not asking about OSA or CPAP compliance — it's a major driver of afib and its recurrence
Not checking magnesium — hypomagnesemia promotes atrial ectopy and makes rate/rhythm control harder
Attributing elevated BNP to CHF without considering the tachycardia itself as the cause — EF may normalize once rate is controlled
Over-treating with amiodarone in the acute setting when rate control alone is sufficient and safer

rateVsRhythm.rateControl: Appropriate first-line for most patients with new-onset afib, especially if duration >48 hours or uncertain. Safer in acute setting. AFFIRM and RACE trials showed rate control is non-inferior to rhythm control for hard outcomes in older patients.
rateVsRhythm.rhythmControl: Consider if: young patient, very symptomatic despite rate control, first episode with clear trigger (may not recur), tachycardia-mediated cardiomyopathy. EAST-AFNET 4 trial showed early rhythm control improved outcomes in patients with recently diagnosed afib and CV risk factors.
rateVsRhythm.forThisPatient: Start with rate control. If LVEF doesn't recover after rate is controlled, or if patient remains very symptomatic, escalate to rhythm control discussion with cardiology. Address modifiable risk factors (OSA, weight, HTN) which may reduce afib burden.

teaching.attendingQuestions: This patient's CHA2DS2-VASc score is 4. Walk me through each component. Now, what anticoagulant do you want to start, at what dose, and why? Also — why did this patient go into afib NOW? What was the trigger, and what modifiable risk factors do you want to address to prevent recurrence?

teachingHooks.ifForgetsAnticoagulation: You've addressed the heart rate, which is great. But what's the biggest long-term danger of atrial fibrillation? How do you assess stroke risk?
teachingHooks.ifWantsToCardiovert: Reasonable thought, but when did symptoms start? If it's been more than 48 hours — or if you're not sure — what do you need to do before cardioversion?
teachingHooks.ifCombinesBBAndCCB: Red flag — you've got the patient on a diltiazem drip AND you're writing for metoprolol. Why is this dangerous?
teachingHooks.ifMissesOSA: The patient snores, has a crowded airway, BMI 34, and uses CPAP inconsistently. How does this connect to his atrial fibrillation?
teachingHooks.ifAsksAboutBNP: Good question. BNP is 280 — is this heart failure? Not necessarily. What else raises BNP? How does the echo help you sort this out?
teachingHooks.ifAsksAboutBetaBlockerWithdrawal: Excellent thinking. He stopped metoprolol 5 days ago. Beta-blocker withdrawal can cause rebound sympathetic surge — tachycardia, hypertension, and can trigger atrial arrhythmias. Never stop beta-blockers abruptly.
teachingHooks.ifAsksAboutApixabanDosing: Great attention to detail. Apixaban has specific dose-reduction criteria — can you name them? Does this patient meet any?
teachingHooks.ifOrdersAspirin: Common misconception. Aspirin is NOT recommended for stroke prevention in atrial fibrillation. The guidelines are clear on this — what should you use instead?
